data.title: AMD Conviction Report
data.description:
CES keynote

AMD (AMD) just used the CES keynote to make a simple point: it’s no longer a parts vendor, it’s a platform company for AI at rack scale, client, and edge, and it now has credible customer pull to match. The star is Helios, an OCP-wide, double-wide rack reference built with AMD’s next-gen MI455 GPUs (2/3nm, HBM4, 3D chiplets) plus Venice EPYC (Zen 6, up to 256 cores) and Pensando networking. Architecturally, this matters because AMD isn’t just selling accelerators; it’s selling a turnkey, open, Ethernet-first system that can be replicated by OEMs/ODMs, exactly where the industry has been struggling to integrate at scale. If you were waiting for AMD’s “DGX moment,” this is it, only done on open standards (UALink over Ethernet, UEC).

Second, the demand validation didn’t come from slides—it came from customers on stage. OpenAI’s Greg Brockman basically said, “we’re compute-constrained,” endorsed co-design on MI455, and described agentic workloads that balloon inference demand. Luma AI (video/multimodal) said ~60% of their inference already runs on AMD, and they’ll 10x deployments; Fei-Fei Li’s World Labs showed spatial/3D models already optimized on MI325X and pushing to MI450. In healthcare, Illumina/AstraZeneca/Absci laid out production use where memory capacity, bandwidth and inference economics—AMD’s strong suits—are gating factors. This is the right kind of proof: real workloads, not just peak TFLOPS.

Third, AMD extended the roadmap credibility: MI455 this year; MI500 (CDNA 6, 2nm, HBM4E) in 2027, claiming a 1,000× four-year curve. On PCs, Ryzen AI 400 and Ryzen AI Max/Halo target local agents with unified memory and a beefy NPU/GPU; Liquid AI’s lightweight multimodal models give a plausible path to on-device AI that isn’t marketing vapor. That broadens TAM and mix, data center remains the profit engine, but AI PCs/edge add unit volume and stickier ecosystems.

Why it matters competitively: Nvidia (NVDA) still leads in frontier training and end-to-end software, but AMD is closing the system integration gap and leaning into openness. Helios plus ROCm day-0 support and rapid cadence lowers the infrastructure adoption barrier; sovereigns and enterprises that don’t want a closed fabric now have a clear alternative. If AMD can marry HBM4 supply (Samsung/Micron) with rack-level tokens-per-watt and tokens-per-dollar leadership on real models, the share narrative changes from “second source” to “second platform.”

What is the price action telling us?

Source: TradingView

AMD’s still trading a box between 200–205 and 228–230. We’re camped in the upper half (~223–224), which is where breakouts are born or fail. The tell is acceptance above 228–230 on closes, not intraday wicks.

Levels that matter (with Fib confluence):

  • Support ladder: 223/222.9 (61.8%) to 212.8 (78.6%) to 205/200 (range floor).
  • Resistance ladder: 228–230 (box top & 50% retrace) to 237.1 (38.2%) to 245.8 (23.6%) to 260 (prior high).
  • If 260 is reclaimed later, extensions line up at 276/297/320.

TPUs change the tape risk, not the thesis:

So far, I believe AMD’s price has been muted mainly because of Google’s TPUs hype. I believe Google does have a very good strategy for inference, but:

  • Google’s TPUs are a credible in-house alternative that create pricing pressure on merchant GPUs and give hyperscalers bargaining leverage. Near term, that can inject headline volatility right at 228–230 (classic “sell-the-breakout” attempts).
  • But TPUs are capacity, power, and portability-constrained and largely cloud-gated. The broader market still needs flexible, merchant, rack-scale GPUs. That dynamic actually helps AMD as the only open, Ethernet-first #2 platform (Helios/MI455, Venice EPYC, ROCm), while it caps NVDA’s pricing umbrella.

What confirms a real breakout (beyond price):

  • Closes >230 on above-average volume plus improving AMD vs NVDA relative strength on your screen.
  • Headlines that speak to merchant demand, not just TPU rhetoric: Helios/MI455 cluster awards, HBM4 allocation momentum, DOE/Genesis Lux/Discovery progress, and cadence in ROCm day-0 model support. Those turn 230 from a lid into a floor.

Bias & playbook (now incorporating TPU noise):

  • Mildly bullish while above 223/222.9. We’re pressing the top rail; momentum’s trying to turn up.
  • Above 230 (close/hold): bulls in control -> targets 237 → 246 → 260. Expect headline feints around TPUs; treat them as noise unless they come with clear signs of HBM diversion away from MI4xx or AMD guide cuts.
  • Fail at 228–230 and lose 222.9: expect a range rotation to 212.8, possibly the 205/200 reload zone. That doesn’t break the secular story; it just resets the swing while the market digests TPU chatter.

Why I’m leaning long into the trigger:

The fundamental tape, OpenAI multi-GW pact, Helios rack-scale unveiling, ROCm cadence, DOE supercomputers, Sanmina NPI partnership, and HBM4 sourcing progress, argues AMD has graduated from chip vendor to platform supplier. TPUs may pressure pricing, but they also force multi-source strategies, and AMD is the merchant alternative customers need.

Bottom line

Respect the box, but I’m a buyer of confirmation, adding on a daily close >230. If TPU headlines ambush the breakout, your line in the sand is 222.9; below that, let it rotate and reset at 212–205 while the long-term thesis stays intact.

Not Financial Advice

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Delek Logistics Partners: Consistent Distribution Growth Tempered By High Leverage
data.description:
Delek Logistics Partners (DKL) is a midstream master limited partnership that primarily operates in Texas and the surrounding states. The company boasts a long history of growth, which has allowed it to be one of the only midstream partnerships to have increased its distribution every quarter for more than a decade. However, the company’s balance sheet appears to be somewhat weaker than its peers, and its distribution coverage is not as strong. The company is growing rapidly, though, and it boasts a 9.27% yield at the current price, so it might be worth considering for an income portfolio.

About Delek Logistics Partners

Delek Logistics Partners is a midstream master limited partnership that primarily transports crude oil, refined products, natural gas, and water. As is the case with many midstream companies, the infrastructure that is owned by Delek Logistics Partners is primarily located in the states of Texas and Oklahoma, although it also has some operations in Arkansas, Louisiana, and New Mexico:

It is not surprising that the company’s infrastructure is primarily located in these states. After all, both Texas and Oklahoma have been the center of the oil and gas industry in the United States for many years. The Permian Basin, located in Texas, is one of the largest hydrocarbon reservoirs in the world, with the most recent estimates stating that the Permian Basin has technically recoverable resources of more than 70 billion barrels of crude oil. The Permian Basin is the largest known deposit of hydrocarbon resources in the continental United States, and despite being exploited since the 1920s, it still contains substantial quantities of resources. The Permian Basin is also one of the most cost-effective basins in the United States in which to produce crude oil. These factors combined have made this region the center of the country’s oil and gas industry, and given the fact that this single basin accounts for more oil and gas production than any other, it makes a lot of sense for a midstream company to have the bulk of its infrastructure located in the Permian Basin and the surrounding areas.

Another reason why Delek Logistics Partners’ focus on Texas and the surrounding states is that it was originally set up by Delek US Holdings (DK) as a vehicle for transporting crude oil and refined products to and from Delek’s refineries. Delek US Holdings owns four refineries in the United States, which are located in Tyler, Texas; Big Spring, Texas; El Dorado, Arkansas; and Krotz Springs, Louisiana. Delek has no refineries outside of those four, so given DKL’s relationship with Delek, it makes sense that DKL’s operations would mostly be in the areas surrounding those four refineries. This is exactly what we see with the company’s operating footprint.

The fact that Delek Logistics Partners was originally established by Delek US Holdings may give some investors concerns about its risks. After all, back in the COVID-19 pandemic, we saw several midstream partnerships that had a larger corporate parent get merged into the corporate parent and potentially hand investors in the partnership losses. BP Midstream Partners was one example of this, as that partnership conducted its IPO in 2017 at a price of $18 per unit, but it was ultimately acquired by BP (BP) for 0.575 ADR shares of BP, which was $17.077 per unit on the April 5, 2022, closing date of the acquisition. There have also been other midstream companies that got rolled back up into the corporate parent over the years, including Rattler Midstream and Shell Midstream. This is not the only risk that investors need to be concerned about when considering purchasing the partnership units of an MLP with a corporate parent. Many of these companies derive a significant proportion of their cash flow from the corporate parent. This makes some sense because the corporate parent frequently sets up a partnership specifically to meet its own logistics needs. There are some risks of having a single entity be responsible for a sizable proportion of the midstream company’s cash flow, though. In particular, if the corporate parent encounters financial difficulties, it could result in adverse consequences for the midstream partnership as well, since the parent might have to either sell some of its position in the midstream partnership to raise cash or have difficulty honoring the contracts that it has with the midstream partnership. Master limited partnerships that are completely independent of a larger company, such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), are sometimes viewed as safer than those that are highly dependent on a single larger company.

Fortunately for investors who are concerned about situations such as the above, Delek Logistics Partners has been working very hard to reduce its dependence on Delek US Holdings for cash flow. Back in 2023, approximately 59% of Delek Logistics Partners’ EBITDA came from Delek US Holdings. That figure declined to 50% for 2024 and is expected to be down to 30% for the full-year 2025 period:

As of the time of writing, we do not know for certain whether or not the company will achieve its goal of deriving 70% of its EBITDA from third-party sources. As of January 15, 2026, Delek Logistics Partners has not announced the date on which it will release its fourth-quarter 2025 and full-year 2025 results, but if history is any guide, it should be sometime around the end of February. For the first nine months of 2025, Delek Logistics Partners had net revenue of $757.557 million. Of this amount, $371.420 million came from Delek US Holdings, and $386.137 million came from outside companies. That is equal to 49.03% from Delek US Holdings. Unless Delek Logistics Partners has substantially higher margins from its third-party contracts than it does from its contracts with Delek US Holdings, it appears to be having problems diversifying its cash flow as much as it expects to. Ultimately, though, money is fungible, and it does not really matter whether the company receives money from Delek US Holdings or an outside company as long as everyone remains financially able to honor the contracts that they have with Delek Logistics Partners. As of the time of writing, there are no fears that customers will be defaulting on their contracts with midstream companies to any significant degree. Many, if not most, upstream and downstream companies are in stronger financial positions than they have been in for many years, and as such, the company’s customers (regardless of whether they are an affiliated party or a third-party company) should be able to continue to pay the money that they have promised to.

In fact, Delek Logistics Partners has been seeing its cash flow increase significantly in recent quarters. For the third quarter of 2025, the company reported an EBITDA of $102.0 million, which represents a massive 47.40% increase over the $69.2 million that the company reported for the third quarter of 2024. One of the reasons for this is that Delek’s crude oil gathering volumes in the Delaware Basin (part of the Permian) set a new record for the third quarter of 2025 with an average of 153,745 barrels of crude oil per day. In the third quarter of 2024, the company’s crude oil gathering pipelines only handled an average of 125,123 barrels of crude oil per day, so the company benefited from a 22.88% increase in gathered crude oil volumes year-over-year.

Delek Logistics’ gathered crude oil volumes are not the only area in which the company has seen improvement compared to the prior-year quarter. We can see that in this chart:

We see that the Midland and the Delaware gathering systems both saw substantial increases year-over-year, with the Delaware gathering system’s volumes setting a new record. The company’s wastewater disposal business is also doing very well, with volumes almost doubling over the past year. This is in line with comments that Delek Logistics Partners’ management made in its third-quarter earnings press release:

During the third quarter Delek Logistics continued its strong execution by making progress on the development of sour gas gathering and acid gas injection capabilities. Delek Logistics also had record crude gathering volumes in its Delaware Business.

The removal of wastewater is a function that many investors do not often associate with midstream companies, even though many of them do it. The oil and gas industry produces a lot of water when engaging in hydraulic fracturing and drilling operations that contain environmental pollutants such as oil, grease, or other chemicals. Environmental regulations require proper disposal of this water, and Delek Logistics Partners provides this service to its customers. This business has been a source of growth for Delek Logistics Partners over the past few years, with its wastewater gathering operations growing from an average of 72,000 barrels per day in 2022 to 616,000 barrels per day in 2025 (a figure that the company expects to increase to 657,000 barrels per day by the end of 2025):

We should note that this has not been entirely organic growth, as some of it was driven by an acquisition that Delek Logistics Partners made last year. On January 2, 2025, Delek Logistics Partners announced that it acquired Gravity Water Intermediate Holdings for $209.3 million in cash, along with 2.175 million common partnership units (valued at about $91 million at the time that the deal was consummated). The partnership units that the company paid for this acquisition are actually worth a bit more now, as the current unit price of $48.14 puts the value of 2.175 million units at $104.7045 million.

The acquisition of Gravity Water Intermediate Holdings had an immediate positive impact on the cash flow and profitability of Delek Logistics Partners. The obvious reason for that is that Gravity Water was cash flow positive at the time of the acquisition, but the benefits extend beyond that. Delek Logistics Partners actually managed to get a pretty good price for the acquisition. The purchase price that Delek Logistics Partners paid was 5.5 times Gravity Water’s EBITDA, which, as regular readers likely know, is in line with the payback period that midstream companies usually attempt to get on their capital investments. For example, this is in line with the 4x to 6x EBITDA multiple that midstream companies typically try to get whenever they are planning to build a new pipeline.

Financial Considerations

Delek Logistics Partners has an incredibly high debt-to-equity ratio, which could pose risks for investors. As of September 30, 2025 (the most recent date for which data is currently available), the company had $2.2890 billion in net debt against $17.5 million in partners’ equity. That is a whopping debt-to-equity ratio of 130.8. As we can see here, this is substantially higher than the figures possessed by some of the other midstream partnerships and corporations in the market:

Company Name

Net Debt-to-Equity Ratio

Delek Logistics Partners

130.8

Enterprise Products Partners

1.12

Energy Transfer (ET)

1.28

MPLX (MPLX)

1.68

Kinder Morgan (KMI)

1.02

The Williams Companies (WMB)

1.88

Targa Resources (TRGP)

6.11

As shown, Delek Logistics Partners has a substantially higher net debt-to-equity ratio than do any of its peer companies. Indeed, even the heavily leveraged Targa Resources is much more conservatively financed than Delek Logistics Partners. This could be a sign that the company is too reliant on debt to finance itself, as the lower ratios of the peer companies suggest that a greater balance between debt and equity financing is generally advisable for risk management purposes.

However, we should note that Delek Logistics Partners has had even weaker balance sheets in the past. As shown here, up until 2024, the company actually had a negative shareholders’ equity:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

A negative partner’ equity symbolizes an undesirable situation in which a company’s liabilities exceed the value of its assets. This is typically the result of losses accumulated over time, which is not necessarily surprising for midstream companies. After all, over the past decade, there were a few instances in which pipeline and energy infrastructure owners wrote down the value of some of their assets. This was an especially common occurrence during the COVID-19 pandemic, for example. With that said, Delek Logistics Partners reported positive net income during all four quarters of 2020, which was much better than some of its peers achieved.

There are other things that can cause negative equity, including distribution payments that exceed the company’s profits. This appears to be the reason for the negative equity in the case of Delek Logistics Partners. This chart shows the company’s annual net income for every year since 2016:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

This chart shows the distributions that Delek Logistics Partners paid out over the same period:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

As we can see, Delek Logistics Partners’ distributions pretty consistently exceed its net income in most years. This does not necessarily mean that the company cannot afford its distributions, however. As midstream companies operate in a capital-intensive and asset-intensive industry, they tend to have high depreciation and amortization expenses. These expenses reduce their reported net income, but they do not actually represent cash leaving the business. A distribution is paid using cash flow, not accounting net income, and looking solely at net income provides a misleading picture of how well a company can afford its distribution.

By the same token, Delek Logistics Partners repays its debt using cash flow, not net income. For the third quarter of 2025, the partnership reported an EBITDA (a proxy for pre-tax cash flow) of $101.978 million. If we annualize that figure, we get $407.912 million. If the company were to use all of its EBITDA to pay down its debt, it would take about 5.61 years. That is still a pretty high figure for a midstream partnership, as companies like Enterprise Products Partners and MPLX have net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratios of less than 4.0x. However, it is not as bad of a situation as we might think from a simple look at the company’s net debt to equity ratio.

Distribution Analysis

Delek Logistics Partners currently pays a distribution of $1.120 per unit quarterly, which gives the company a massive 9.27% yield at the current share price. The most recent distribution is an increase over the $1.115 per share distribution that it paid for the second quarter of 2025, and it represents the company’s 51st consecutive quarterly distribution increase:

This is something of a rarity among midstream partnerships. Unlike some of the other companies in the sector, Delek Logistics Partners did not cut its distribution following the industry crises that occurred following the crude oil price collapses in 2014 and 2020. In fact, it has one of the best track records of any midstream partnership as far as its distribution is concerned. However, the fact that the company has raised its distribution every quarter for more than ten straight years does not necessarily mean that it can actually afford the distribution that it pays out. Let us take a closer look at its finances to see how well it is covering the payout.

For the third quarter of 2025, Delek Logistics Partners reported that it had a distributable cash flow of $73.584 million. That was sufficient to cover the distribution that the company declared 1.23 times over, which is, admittedly, tighter than we would really like to see. Wall Street analysts usually consider anything higher than 1.20x to be sustainable over the long term, and Delek Logistics Partners does meet that criterion. However, I am somewhat more conservative than Wall Street analysts and typically like to see this ratio be above 1.30x in order to add a margin of safety to the position. Many of the partnerships that are recommended here at Energy Profits in Dividends have distribution coverage ratios of 1.30x or higher. Delek Logistics Partners does not satisfy this requirement, and as such, there may be higher risks to the distribution here than there would be with some other midstream companies. Potential investors may want to keep this in mind before purchasing the partnership units of this company.

Valuation

Delek Logistics Partners does not appear to be particularly expensive right now. Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system assigns the company a valuation grade of B:

However, we do notice that some of the company’s valuation ratios are significantly above the energy sector median. In particular, the enterprise value-to-EBITDA is rather high. Here is how it compares to some of the peer companies:

Company Name

Quant Valuation Grade

EV/EVITDA (FWD)

Delek Logistics Partners

B

9.62

Enterprise Products Partners

10.66

Energy Transfer

8.49

MPLX

11.53

Kinder Morgan

11.43

The Williams Companies

D-

13.50

D-

Overall, Delek Logistics Partners seems to compare pretty well with its peers. It has the second-lowest EV/EBITDA ratios, and it is one of only two companies to have earned a B rating from the entire valuation system.

In short, Delek Logistics Partners does not appear to be overpriced at the current level.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Delek Logistics Partners is a high-yielding midstream partnership that has a long history of increasing its distribution on a quarterly basis. These quarterly increases may be particularly attractive to those income-seeking investors who wish to ensure that their incomes keep up with inflation. However, the company is quite highly leveraged, and its distribution coverage is not as strong as some of its peers, so there could be some risks here. The company has historically been fairly good at delivering growth, however, and its valuation is reasonable, so it might be worth considering for an income portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Broadcom's AI Upgrade Is Earned, The Valuation Already Knows
data.description:
I tried plotting both a Buy and a Sell thesis for Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) and failed to generate conviction in either. The bottom line is that I have to echo the sentiments of the Seeking Alpha Quant Rating for Broadcom and sit on the fence. And exactly for the reasons the quant rating indicates. Broadcom now shows up as a stronger business compared to a couple of years ago, driven by AI infrastructure demand and a scaled software platform. So while it scores really well on growth and margin counts, valuations are fair at best and already capture the positive changes.

The growth duration and new cyclical floor in the AI era are yet to be validated, and valuations remain sensitive to even a couple of quarters of slower-than-expected AI capex. At the same time, strong fundamentals, backlog visibility, and margin resilience make a sell premature.

The only change I would make from how the quant ratings have shaped up is that I would have treated even $200 as a good entry point (in hindsight and otherwise) and anything above $400 as an opportunistic Sell point (if accompanied by no material fundamental change).

Valuation Vs. Cash Flow Reality

Broadcom's TTM price to FCF ratio is now at ~61x after corrections from ~75x over the past couple of months. While the TTM measures show a 2-3x valuation expansion since 2024, a lot of it is earned by a step jump in the forward outlook. The price-to-forward operating cash flow is only 40% up from its 5-year average levels. This, of course, includes the last two years of breakaway valuation expansion too, but it works out to be a far lower expansion than the TTM metrics suggest. The price-to-TTM operating cash flow valuation also includes the last couple of years of expanding valuations, but shows over 100% valuation expansion over the 5-year average (in line with the P to FCF charts below).

The valuations look less extreme when looking at the earned FCF expansion trajectory in the past couple of years. Extrapolating the TTM FCF trajectory in the pre-AI era, we may have seen somewhere around $21b annualized FCF levels today. The measurable AI uplift and a VMWare-enabled stronger software earnings have accounted for a significant lift to ~$26b on a TTM basis. That alone justifies a bulk of P to FCF valuation rerating. The remaining part of the rerating can be attributed to expectations of a lower cyclical floor going ahead due to the same levers that have helped in the past couple of years.

While the FCF trajectory is materially altered, there are two issues that I find blocking a Buy, given the current valuation levels. One is the lack of data in terms of AI-era stress (likely capex pause or deceleration) and how it impacts the cyclicality of FCFs going ahead. And two, a lack of any significant FCF margin expansion in the same two-year period of FCF growth. In fact, FCF margins have actually shrunk in this regime.

The gross margins are expected to be negatively impacted due to the AI expansion. This is now evident from the FCF graph above as much as from management's Q1 guidance (issued in Q4 FY2025) -

We expect Q1 consolidated gross margin to be down approximately 100 basis points sequentially, primarily reflecting a higher mix of AI revenue

An absolute growth in FCF in a regime of lower gross margins reinforces that valuation support comes from scale, not margin surprise.

How Has Growth Profile Changed

In 2025, AI revenue has grown 65% YoY to $20b, and management has guided a near 100% YoY growth in AI revenues for Q1 FY2026. Broadcom's growth profile change in this era is still heavily AI-driven and dependent. Non-AI semis revenue outlook is flat and stable for 2026. Infrastructure software is growing at low double digits.

The dependency on the AI tailwinds (almost completely) does not mean caution straightaway because there are no signs of demand exhaustion yet, and guidance for 2026, in fact, shows we are still in the accelerating half of the cycle.

What blocks a Buy, though, is that while management broadly guided for the full year in the case of infrastructure software and non-AI semis, it did not issue a full-year outlook for AI demand. This is the key growth pillar, as already evident from the segment-level revenue growth numbers and uncertainties around a moving backlog and unpredictable cadence, showing how the economics are probably durable but may lack linearity.

AI Backlog, Visibility, and Concentration

Broadcom ended FY2025 with an AI backlog of ~$73b, which accounted for 45% of total backlog. The delivery window is 6 quarters. Of this backlog, around $53b is tied to XPUs, with the remaining ~$20b coming from networking, optics, and DSPs. The order book is expectedly concentrated with multiple single customer orders above $10b. Demand visibility extends beyond the 6 quarters with a fifth AI customer and OpenAI system-level deals flowing into the 2027-29 window as well.

While visibility is great, linearity is not the same as a subscription-based revenue stream. Concentration makes risks higher and revenue trajectories lumpier at the very least. And management's clarification on why a longer-term guidance becomes difficult is well explained (a happy problem):

Our backlog is very dynamic these days, as I said, and it is continuing to ramp up. And you're right, we originally, 6 months ago, said, maybe year-on-year AI revenues would grow in '26, 60%, 70%. Q1, we doubled. And Q1 '26 today, we're saying it's doubled. And we're looking at it because all the fresh orders keep coming in, and we give you a milestone of where we are today, which is $73 billion of backlog to be shipped over the next 18 months. And we do fully expect, as I answered the earlier question, for that $73 billion over the 18 months to keep growing.

Now it's a moving number as we move in time, but it will grow. And it's hard for me to pinpoint what '26 is going to look like precisely. So I'd rather not give you guys any guidance, and that's why we don't give you guidance, but we do give it for Q1. Give it time, we'll give it for Q2.

The backlog is strong, but not smooth—that's the new reality. While that justifies valuations, it also means more execution and more data should be awaited to draw trend lines and reward AVGO further rerating—valuation upside should be capped from here on at least.

A Classic Hold

Finally, Broadcom is increasingly selling systems and racks, not just chips. These include XPUs, networking, optics, DSPs, and PCIe components. The advanced packaging supply chain is becoming more robust. This strategic shift toward system-level selling increases revenue per deployment and increases customer stickiness.

It also means more pass-through components and higher reinvestment needs. This explains why FCF margins have not risen as much as absolute dollar cash flows have. That too, like several other points discussed in the thesis remains supportive for valuations but makes it hard to envision further multiple expansion from here on.

Overall, Broadcom is a classic example of a hold—it's best to pass than trying to chase or time. Those who could time entries in 2024-25 should stick, but fresh entries seem to lack high-conviction arguments either way. I acknowledge that the bus has been missed, and the odds of a favorable multiple compression while the fundamental outlook remains intact are low. If valuation compresses toward ~25x on the price-to-forward operating cash flow scale, I will evaluate fresh entries—but I doubt that will come without a dampener like signs of cyclicality that alter the fundamental thesis as well. I will be more comfortable issuing a Sell if Broadcom crosses $400, as rerating triggers appear less possible, and therefore $400 could open up decent risk-reward asymmetry favoring shorts on valuations alone.

data.title: EM Debt Outlook 2026: Momentum Continues
data.description:
Momentum continues in emerging markets (EM) debt, and we believe the forces behind it haven’t faded. Solid growth, improving balance sheets, attractive real yields, and renewed inflows have carried strength from 2025 into the year ahead. But first, let’s look back at 2025.

Strong Performance in 2025

EM debt had a strong year in 2025. The asset class delivered robust returns across the board, with credit spreads in hard currency corporates and sovereigns tightening and yields moving lower. In the local currency space, EM currencies appreciated while local interest rates declined. And throughout the year, EM debt benefited from a combination of resilient fundamentals, strong technical conditions, and supportive valuations. All this resulted in strong performance across sub-asset classes.

A Stable Macroeconomic Backdrop

Despite rising concerns about potential impacts from global trade tensions driven by U.S. policies and ongoing geopolitical conflicts, the overall fundamental backdrop remains resilient, and we believe this resilience should persist into 2026.

We believe EM economies will grow at approximately 3.7% on a gross domestic product (GDP)-weighted basis next year, as shown in the chart below, broadly in line with 2025’s estimated 3.9%, which exceeded expectations following concerns raised by the April 2025 U.S. tariff announcements.

This solid economic growth should continue to anchor fiscal and debt dynamics across most EMs, where we anticipate stable fiscal positions and debt-to-GDP ratios. Furthermore, AI-related productivity gains should further support growth dynamics in many EMs.

External accounts are a particularly bright spot, where we believe persistent capital flows, strong direct foreign investment, and healthy current account surpluses will lead to robust balance-of-payments positions. The inflationary backdrop is benign, which should allow EM central banks to continue cutting rates, providing additional support going forward.

Resilient GDP Growth in EM Economics

We expect EM GDP growth to remain steady across regions, holding near 4% through the forecast period. Asia and Africa continue to lead. While growth moderates slightly from 2025 onward, the trajectory remains stable, reinforcing the resilience of EM economies.

In 2025, the effective U.S. tariff rate on imported goods saw one of its largest increases in more than a century. However, by the end of 2025, the impact was less severe than many had forecast in April, and reciprocal tariffs announced were softened by exemptions for critical goods and subsequent adjustments. Although risks related to U.S. tariff policy and global trade are ongoing, EMs have become increasingly insulated, with intra-EM trade now accounting for more than 50% of total EM trade, up from roughly 25% in the early 2000s, as shown in the chart below. This significantly reduces EM reliance on advanced economies, particularly the United States.

While global growth may decelerate due to softer conditions in developed markets, we believe EM fundamentals are resilient enough to withstand this gradual deceleration and credit default risk is expected to remain well contained. Overall, we view EM fundamentals as constructive for continued strong performance.

Intra-EM Trade Rising

Intra-EM trade now accounts for more than 50% of total EM trade, up from roughly 25% in the early 2000s. This significantly reduces EM reliance on advanced economies, particularly the United States.

Sources: Direction of Trade Statistics (DOTS) and William Blair. Data is from March 31, 1970, through June 30, 2025, quarterly. DOTS presents the value of merchandise exports and imports disaggregated according to a country’s primary trading partners. Area and world aggregates are included in the display of trade flows between major areas of the world. Reported data is supplemented by estimates whenever such data is not available or current. Imports are reported on a cost, insurance and freight (CIF) basis and exports are reported on a free on board (FOB) basis, with the exception of a few countries for which imports are also available FOB. Time series data includes estimates derived from reports of partner countries for non-reporting and slow-reporting countries.

Supportive Technical Conditions

We expect technical conditions to remain supportive. Net new debt issuance should stay contained, below historical averages, and bilateral and multilateral institutions should continue to provide ample and affordable funding to EMs, reducing the need for issuance of new debt. At the same time, 2025 marked a shift in flows, with EM debt posting its first year of inflows since 2021. Global fixed-income investors remain underinvested in the asset class, creating room for increased allocations. Looking ahead, we anticipate a favorable backdrop for continued allocations into dedicated EM debt strategies in 2026.

Compelling Valuations

Valuations across EM debt remain supportive as we head into 2026.

In hard currency, credit spreads are tight but yields remain above long-term averages. We expect overall index-level spreads to tighten toward the 250-basis-point mark, driven primarily by further narrowing in high-yield credits. We believe this backdrop, combined with an expected decline in U.S. Treasury yields (we expect the 10-year to reach 3.5% over the next 12 months), should support compelling returns for the J.P. Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index (EMBI) Global Diversified.

In local currency, we believe investors are underestimating the potential for further monetary easing and see significant value in local rates. Attractive valuations suggest room for currency appreciation, as the U.S. dollar remains in overvalued territory. We expect EM currencies to perform well against the dollar and currencies of other developed markets. Spot rates versus purchasing power parity and real yields (as shown in the charts below) continue to highlight opportunities.

Spot Currency Deviations From PPP Conversion Rate Per Dollar

EM currencies appear undervalued relative to the U.S. dollar based on PPP deviations, while most developed market currencies hover near fair value or slightly overvalued. We expect EM currencies to perform well against the dollar and those of other developed markets.

Sources: Bloomberg, Oxford Economics, and William Blair, as of October 31, 2025.

Developed, Emerging, and Frontier Market Real Policy Rates

Emerging and frontier markets generally exhibit substantially higher trailing and forecast real yields compared to developed markets, where real yields remain near zero or negative. This disparity highlights a potential advantage for investors seeking positive real returns, as EM and frontier economies may offer more attractive income opportunities relative to developed markets.

Sources: Bloomberg, Oxford Economics, and William Blair, as of October 31, 2025.

A Strong Case for Frontier Markets

We also believe frontier markets remain attractive, supported by resilient fundamentals, high real yields, and ongoing foreign exchange adjustments.

While the potential for further rate cuts is generally limited after aggressive easing in 2025, select countries—including Egypt, Ghana, Zambia, and Kenya—have room for additional monetary easing as inflation moderates and external balances improve. In Nigeria, we believe further cuts are likely to be gradual and data-dependent rather than substantial. While the potential for further rate cuts elsewhere is more limited, strong carry, improving fiscal dynamics, and robust external accounts—especially among commodity exporters—should sustain investor interest.

Despite tighter spreads and concentrated positioning in a few markets, we believe the risk/reward profile is still positive, with most frontier debt offering a meaningful spread pickup over sovereign and developed market peers, and systemic risks contained by resilient fundamentals.

Diverse Opportunities in Corporate Credit

We also believe EM corporate credit continues to offer myriad investment opportunities at incremental spreads to sovereign credit at specific durations.

Issuance in 2025 rebounded to its highest level since 2021, still driven by supply outside of China. Expectations are for issuance to increase modestly into 2026, with net financing still marginally negative. Default expectations of 3% rise modestly for next year but are still below historical norms. With top-level credit metrics only marginally weaker, but still stronger than those of developed markets, we believe it is unlikely systemic risks will occur as fundamentals remain resilient.

Lastly, while the index-level valuation conundrum continues, with historically low spreads but still attractive all-in yield, corporate credit continues to offer a spread pickup when compared to its country of risk.

The Oil Outlook

In 2025, oil markets were pressured by constant oversupply expectations and global trade concerns, with bouts of geopolitics providing price spikes. Prices are closing the year at about $65 per barrel, the lower end of the yearly range.

Demand for crude is expected to grow by approximately 1 million barrels per day in 2025, a bit lower than historical levels, but still decent, although some of this demand went into strategic inventories in China. Supply created an imbalance, with additional barrels coming from U.S. shale, Brazil, Guyana, Canada, Norway, and the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC).

Visible inventories have been built, and floating storage seems to be absorbing sanctioned barrels, which eventually will need to hit the market. So, in 2026, fundamentals point to an oversupplied market. With expectations of more than 2 million barrels per day of oversupply, prices should remain under pressure even as demand remains steady.

Given this, we remain cautious on oil-related investments, but also note that low-visibility events such as geopolitics or OPEC’s (and others’) reactions to much lower prices could offset the visible imbalance and provide some price support. Moreover, a resolution or escalation of geopolitical conflicts—such as those in Ukraine, Iran, or Venezuela—would likely impact global oil markets.

Notably, recent updates to longstanding forecasts propose steadier crude demand growth into the foreseeable future, with some even suggesting a supply crunch stemming from decline rates (the natural rate at which production declines from an existing oil or gas field) and the investment cycle later this decade.

The Metals Outlook

We experienced an exceptional year for metals in 2025, with precious metals leading the charge. Gold and silver reached all-time highs driven by investor flows amid geopolitical uncertainty and Fed rate cuts. Gold was also supported by central bank purchases and silver by liquidity squeezes resulting from tariff concerns and inventory builds in the United States. Platinum benefited from gold’s gravitational pull, illiquidity in non-U.S. markets, and supply tightness.

Meanwhile, 2025 industrial metal performance was more diverse as copper outperformed on strong energy transition demand and supply downturns while aluminum gains were more modest given the metal's ample supply. Nickel’s oversupply woes continued throughout the year.

For 2026, we believe that precious metals will remain supportive, driven by the Fed rate-cut cycle, geopolitical uncertainty, and fiscal deficits. In our opinion, gold should continue to benefit from ongoing central-bank buying, which we view as a more structural source of demand. Meanwhile, silver’s liquidity concerns could ease if tariff risks subside for the metal.

Industrial metals, meanwhile, could see continued momentum from major themes such as electrification and the expansion of AI and data centers, and cyclical demand might return. We remain cautious on Chinese demand growth in 2026 due to policy changes and economic uncertainty. However, nickel’s supply-demand balance could improve if the Indonesian government follows through on scaling back supply incentives and energy allocation shifts toward other metals, such as aluminum.

A Bright Outlook

All in all, we anticipate another strong year for EM debt in 2026. While risks remain—stemming from U.S. policy uncertainty, trade tensions, and ongoing geopolitical conflicts—we believe these risks should stay relatively well contained.

A constructive backdrop for performance stems from resilient fundamentals anchored by solid GDP growth, stable fiscal positions, and benign inflation; valuations supported by attractive real yields and currency upside; and technical conditions reinforced by contained net new debt issuance and asset class inflows.

Pragmatism from U.S. policymakers and steady global engagement reinforce our view that the opportunities outweigh the risks, and we are positioned accordingly to capture upside potential across both hard and local currency markets.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were added by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Applied Digital: The REIT Angle No One Talks About
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The present article examines the possibilities of Applied Digital’s (NASDAQ: APLD) stock continuing to deliver enormous returns for its holders.

APLD’s stock achieved an impressive return of over 300%, mainly due to its transition from a mining company to a data center lease company. APLD is in a growth phase and remains unprofitable, while its valuation metrics signals an overvalued company.

Growth catalysts of modular data center design, in combination with their strategic location and the high demand for AI data centers, overcome the company’s main risks of execution and financial sensitivity.

Finally, I explain why investors should soon evaluate the company as a REIT and not as an AI data center developer; as a result, I rate the stock as BUY.

Solid Financial Overview and Financing Strategy

APLD has two businesses: the Data Center Hosting Business, which provides infrastructure services to crypto mining customers, and the HPC Hosting Business (or HPC), which designs and provides data centers for AI workloads. APLD’s business model is to build data centers and rent them to hyperscalers or to mining companies.

Let’s break down the company’s Q2’26 results. APLD reported YoY revenue growth of 250% reaching $126.6M in the last quarter. Data Center Hosting generated only $5.4M, or about 4% of total revenue, while the rest $85M came from HPC Hosting.

From the above $85M revenue the $73M are regarding tenant fit-out services -which means workloads to tailor the data center in the needs of the client- but the cost of revenues (COR) related to tenant fit-out workloads reached to $69.5M, signaling that this segment delivers almost zero profit in practice.

Overall, COR increased YoY by 344% and the main driver was the fit-out workloads; however, this means that APLD is currently in “build” phase of the data centers, and this process needs massive expenses, resulting in reduced profitability, specifically to an operating loss of $2.6M (from a loss of $5.8M in Q2’25).

The takeaway here is that tenant fit-out workloads may have low or zero profit margin but they the first step before the company is able to rent its data centers and truly generate significant profits.

Therefore, the company’s net loss of about $19M in Q2’26 doesn’t reflect its future potential. Given that net loss for Q2’25 was $139.3M, I believe its strategic shift to the development of AI-related data centers will boost its revenue and profitability in the long term, and the breakeven point is just a matter of time.

APLD has long term debt of about $2.6B, while it reported a net loss and needs high investments in CapEx -as we will see later- to support its growth initiatives.

As a growth company within an asset-heavy business model, the company needs high investments to support its growth; therefore, its ability for flexible financing is crucial. In detail, the company’s management stated:

The first step of this process is to draw on our development loan facility with Macquarie Equipment Capital, which allows us to fund pre-leased construction for new sites... The second step, following a mutually agreed upon executed lease with an investment-grade hyperscaler is to access the Macquarie Asset Management's $5 billion preferred equity facility…This multilayered financing framework allows Applied Digital to leverage third-party capital for a majority of the upfront investment…On the debt front this quarter, we completed a $2.35 billion private offering of our 9.25% senior secured notes due 2030 to finance the first 2 -- 2 of the 3 buildings at our Polaris Forge 1 site.

To make it simple, the company uses:

  1. Short-term loans for pre-leased data centers, which means lower interest for these loans as the revenue from these data centers is “locked”.
  2. Preferred Equity totaling of $5B from Macquarie Asset Management; APLD has drawn only $900M from this facility to support the development of Polaris Forge 1 and 2 campuses
  3. Issuing new debt of $2.35B to support the first two buildings at Polaris Forge 1 site

In my view, the positive is that most debt ($2.7B) has maturity in 2030, which gives flexibility to the company in order to support its current high investment needs and time to accumulate cash to pay the debt. given the high rate of 9.25%. I believe that when the construction period is finished and its data centers are leased and generate revenue, the company may seek for refinancing of this debt with lower rate. Overall, its financing methods do not put high pressure on the company’s financial position, considering its needs for high CapEx.

Growth Catalysts

Let’s examine the company’s bull case.

Hyperscalers Demand

According to the company’s CEO statement:

Having secured two hyperscale leases in the region, inbound demand has increased meaningfully… As a result, we are in advanced discussions with another investment-grade hyperscaler across multiple regions, including additional locations in the Dakotas and select Southern U.S. markets.

In short, the CEO points out that demand for the company’s data centers is high and that the company is currently in discussions with a new hyperscaler in order to lease them sites.

Modular data center design and their strategic location

Starting again with the company’s CEO statement, regarding modularity:

Our current data center designs are modular and highly efficient, allowing us to run numerous concrete plants simultaneously and leverage prefabricated components delivered by 18-wheelers… We've expanded the footprint and flexibility of our buildings designed to allow for different GPU and ASIC chip architectures and networking infrastructure to support multipurpose AI use cases and traditional cloud workloads.

I believe that the company means that it constructs its data center with prefabricated components, which means faster construction and fewer delays. Also, they can build many sites in parallel which reduced the time-to-market. In addition, its data centers could support various types of workloads, from traditional clouds to high performance AI-related.

Regarding strategic location of its data centers:

…we chose the Dakotas because we believe they provide a durable competitive advantage with low cost of abundant energy, [ new ] climate, ample land for expansion of existing sites and potential for future large-scale super sites that could align with regional energy developments.

As the image below shows, Dakota offers low energy costs and has already have advanced connectivity. I can’t say that this location is a competitive advantage by itself; however, I believe that both of these factors could support the company’s growth.

In my opinion, modular data centers and their strategic location is a combination that could attract new hyperscalers. Given that the company delivered on time and on budget for CoreWeave’s (CRWV) sites, enhanced my bull case.

Tracking NOI and Risks

Let me analyze the company’s milestone statement of $1B in Net Operating Income (NOI). The company stated:

We now expect to surpass our long-term goal of $1 billion in NOI within 5 years.

Currently, the company expects to generate $16B in lease revenue for APLD over 15 years and to ramp up over the next 18-24 months based on the remaining capacity under construction. But why estimate NOI of $1B when it currently operates with loss?

I based my analysis on the financial information below. These estimates assume that the company will fully develop the PF1 campus by the end of 2027 and PF2 by the end of 1H27, according to the appendix below; and will be in a position to fully lease the capacity.

Breaking down APLD’s estimations, I conclude that from PF1 site the company needs about $4.4–$5.2 billion in CapEx, in order to generate annual revenue of $730M. Applying an NOI of about 88% the annualized NOI is calculated at $640M.

Regarding the PF2, using the same approach, the annualized NOI is $287M. In total, the company estimates that both sites are expected to generate about $927M.

In my view, APLD plans to build new sites of 400-600 MW in order to generate NOI of $300-$500 million, assuming the same level of 88% NOI margin as above. So, its estimation of $1B NOI, given the high demand and its growth catalysts, is achievable in my view.

My concern is that the company needs to spend massive amounts on CapEx upfront in order to build the campuses. Also, the company builds its sites without having contracts for all of its capacity; therefore, the execution risk is high. Any contract cancellation or delay of a data center could impact the company’s performance.

Given that APLD needs CapEx of about $6.6-$7.8 billion upfront, and still remains unprofitable with negative FCF, leverage and liquidity risks also arise. In my opinion, this is something normal for growth companies like APLD, and investors should focus more on its ability to attract new customers and deliver projects on time rather than its current stretched financial position. Competition, as well as high revenue concentration, are also significant risks that investors should take into account.

Overall, the company may estimate NOI of over $1B but its contracts and its construction progress are still in early stage. I anticipate APLD announcing more contracts with hyperscalers, but its financial and operational position remains under pressure.

REIT-Based Valuation Reflects Undervaluation

Considering that APLD is unprofitable, I base my valuation analysis on revenue growth, as well as sales-related ratios.

In terms of revenue growth, analysts predict an impressive trajectory, making its FWD P/S ratio more than attractive.

Traditional sales-related valuation metrics signal an overvalued stock, which is normal for early-stage companies like APLD.

The above metrics give mixed valuation signals, and its risk-growth comparison is too sensitive, for investors to have a clear opinion on whether its current price is attractive or not.

Trying to discover a factor that will clarify the valuation landscape, I found that APLD aims to be a data center REIT in the future. This means that if I value the company as a future REIT, I can assume higher multipliers and it will attract new types of investors who focus on dividends and have a low risk-tolerance profile, which can boost APLD’s stock price. Notably, data center REIT companies such as Digital Realty Trust (DLR) and Equinix (EQIX) have stock prices of about $159 and $800 respectively.

I do not anticipate APLD reaching these price levels -at least not soon; however, I anticipate that when investors understand that APLD is not just a data center constructor and actually could be a REIT, its stock price could deliver a solid return to investors. I want to note that this is a long-term target -in my view over 3 years- and investors should be patient. As evidence that APLD aims to be a REIT in reality, I point to the recent spin-out of its Applied Digital Cloud business, which is not a lease-oriented revenue source.

Given all the above, I rate the stock as BUY.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: An AI Value Play
data.description:
Introduction

It has been a while since I last reviewed Kinder Morgan (KMI) and gave it a Hold rating back in early April. I believe that my pick was right on the money, as the company saw a slight stock decline of 0.65%, while it has capitalized on significant demand in natural gas for various needs such as data centers. I want to dive into the company to see if it now has what it takes to garner a Buy rating.

Right now, Kinder Morgan is one of the largest infrastructure companies in the US with around 79,000 miles of pipelines that transport about 40% of all natural gas produced in the country

Current Dynamics

Kinder Morgan has been one of the big winners of the energy surge for the AI and data center revolution, as the company has reported a massive $9.3B project backlog. KMI is pivoting its infrastructure to serve the massive 24/7 power needs of hyperscale data centers as the company is pursuing over 10 Bcf/d of new opportunities for natural gas power generation. Half of its current sanctioned projects are related to this, which is a drastic difference from years prior, where LNG was the sole driver.

This is why KMI’s backlog is made up of 90% of natural gas projects, with key endeavors including the Trident Intrastate pipeline that will move 2 Bcf/d as well as the South System Expansion 4 that will add 1.3 Bcf/d of capacity. With all of these projects under construction, KMI can expect to increase its volume towards LNG facilities to 12 Bcf/d by 2028, up from 8 Bcf/d.

On the data center front, KMI agreed to provide gas transmission services as part of a $55B framework led by Mitsubishi Electric, where KMI is slated to provide the infrastructure for a $7B portion of this framework. The massive project is essentially dedicated to building and fueling power station systems. KMI is also undergoing the Elba Express Bridge project, that has been fast-tracked in late 2025 to serve the South Carolina data center corridor.

I also like what KMI has been doing towards the energy transition with its low-carbon ventures. Gas is certainly the core of the company, but KMI is also investing in profitable transition projects, as it is one of the largest RNG producers in the country. Kinetrex, a landfill-based facility, is set to come online with a goal of reaching 6.9 Bcf/ year of gross production.

KMI has also seen an upgrade in its credit rating to BBB+ by Fitch and S&P, and though I would generally not make a headline out of it, this is crucial for large infrastructure companies like midstream ones, as they generally need debt to finance their large project endeavor such as the ones that KMI has been realizing. On the side of S&P, it has noted that KMI’s leverage ratio is currently at the bottom of the historical range, as well as the fact that the 79,000 miles of pipe are virtually irreplaceable assets in the current energy market.

As the company’s next quarter release is fast approaching, I want to highlight what I’ll be looking for. I’ll be looking closely at the project backlog, with first, if it has grown as executives have hinted at a shadow backlog, and especially if the power and data centers now represent more than 50% of the backlog. I’ll also listen for new FIDs on any pipelines, which could mean that the AI supercycle is accelerating. Specifically, I want to hear further details about the timing of the $7B power station partnership with Mitsubishi.

Valuation

I’ll say that KMI’s valuation is relatively in line with peers, with its EV/EBITDA of 13.87, below Williams & Company and Enbridge. It has a relatively fair FCF yield, though it has dropped compared to the company’s historical average, as I believe that the market is pricing that KMI is in a prime position to capitalize on the current surge in demand from AI. Now, how does the company justify the premium over other well-established companies like ET and EPD that have a large diversified source of income stream? First, KMI is a regular C-Corp that adds an accessibility premium, as many investors do not want to deal with the K1 hassle. Also, KMI is deemed the cleanest play for the AI power revolution thanks to its natural gas dominance, as well as its geographic advantage, as KMI’s network is heavily concentrated towards the Southeast and Texas. The current dividend yield is standing at 4.3%, which is on the lower side of the industry, probably due to its C-Corp status, and its growth is currently only at 2.35% CAGR over the past 5 years, which is significantly inferior compared to peers.

Looking ahead, the company is expecting adj. EPS of $1.37, which would be an 8% increase over 2025 and adj. EBITDA is expected to reach $8.7B, which would also be a 4% increase. Looking at CapEx, management is planning to invest $3.4B in discretionary growth projects. This clearly gives us a roadmap ahead, as the theme of the company going into 2026 would be aggressive execution of their project backlog, especially as AI is all the rage on the market. Though one thing that the market is further rewarding would be the cautious self-fund approach, as the company stated they will no longer tap the equity or the debt markets, which is certainly different from its peers.

KMI also generally fares better than its peers in terms of profitability, with EBITDA margin standing at 49.12% and net at 16.61%. It is also important to grasp how KMI gets its earnings with around 90% predictability, as roughly 64% of the company’s cash flow is take-or-pay based and another 26% is fee-based. Regarding commodity insulation, only 5% of KMI’s EBITDA is directly exposed to the fluctuating price of oil and gas, allowing KMI to retain the same levels of profitability during market downturns. The high-quality investments are mostly under take-or-pay contracts with blue-chip customers. I also expect margins to increase as intrastate pipelines generally have short terms and market-based pricing and seeing how demand is skyrocketing for KMI, fees will certainly increase in the coming years.

Risks

Valuation could be a certain risk as the company has a slight premium over the industry based on its position within the AI supercycle. If there is a slowdown perceived in the next earnings call, this could trigger a re-rating for KMI’s stock.

And though it is currently operating in a favorable regulatory environment, the major projects remain vulnerable to legal challenges. For example, the South System Expansion 4 is facing organized opposition from environmental groups like the Alabama Rivers Alliance and Energy Alabama. The main issue would be that the project would have an impact on water quality in the Black Belt region.

Conclusion

Kinder Morgan is certainly an intriguing play right now in an industry that I’m certainly bullish on, as it is heavily tilted towards natural gas and the AI play. Would I pick it over companies such as ET and EPD? I don’t know, but what it allows us to do is bet on the AI infrastructure race at a relatively cheap valuation plus a nice yield. I upgrade my rating to a Buy for Kinder Morgan.

data.title: PepsiCo Vs. Coca-Cola: Battle Of The Low Beta Beverage Stocks
data.description:
Introduction

While fancy tech names like Nvidia, Tesla, and Alphabet tend to dominate investors’ attention, strong portfolios still depend on owning low-beta businesses that generate consistent cash flows across cycles, in addition to higher-risk growth stocks. PepsiCo (PEP) and The Coca-Cola Company (KO) are two of the most common portfolio choices for this purpose. But which is better value? In this article, I argue that KO is the superior choice. For investors looking to lower portfolio beta, KO offers a more compelling choice due to business model efficiency, dividend durability, and valuation support.

Business Comparison

Business Model and Asset Intensity

Pepsi and Coke have very clearly differentiated business strategies. The financial ratios for each company help tell this story:

Metric PEP KO
Revenue $92.3 billion $47.6 billion
Profit Margin 7.82% 27.34%
Operating Margin

16.9%

 32.37%
R.O.A. 8.37% 8.96%
R.O.E. 37.16% 42.44%
Current Ratio 0.91 1.21
Net PPE $31.4 billion $10.3 billion
Net PPE/Revenue 34% 21.6%

PEP has high revenue, lower profit margins, and slightly lower ROA and ROE than KO. Additionally, PEP has more than 3 times the balance sheet value of property, plant, and equipment. This indicates that PEP operates a more capital-intensive business model - with a larger and more diversified group of products - than KO does. PEP has a number of brands that each generate $1 billion or more in revenue, according to the company's latest 10K filing, including the flagship brand Pepsi-Cola, Lay's Potato Chips, Gatorade, Mountain Dew, and Doritos. KO, although it also owns many beverage brands, is much more reliant on its core brands: Coca-Cola, Coca-Cola Zero Sugar, Sprite, and Fanta. The company is much less diversified into products like snack foods than PepsiCo is. While Pepsi owns the entire supply chain, Coca-Cola outsources the bottling of its drinks to bottling companies - it sells them the syrup to make the drinks.

So why does the business model matter? Because Coca-Cola can fund growth more efficiently. For Pepsi to grow, it needs to invest in more factories to make all kinds of different products and expand supply chains, which involves either reinvesting cash generated from operations or issuing bonds. Coca-Cola can grow organically as its customer base grows, which occurs naturally as consumers in emerging markets gain more purchasing power. Therefore, Coca-Cola doesn't have to dip into its dividend-paying potential to fund growth the way Pepsi has to.

Business model winner: Coca-Cola

Capital Structure

The differences in business models are also reflected in the capital structure of the two companies, shown in the table below.

Metric PEP KO
Book Value of Debt $51 billion $48 billion
Market Value of Equity $202 billion $307 billion
Debt-to-Equity Ratio 25.2% 15.6%

Pepsi has more debt than Coca-Cola has with a lower market value, giving it a debt-to-equity ratio of 25.2% vs. 15.6% for KO. This means that in addition to PEP's already greater capital needs, it also already has more leverage, making extra leverage to fund growth more expensive. Both bondholders and stockholders are likely to require a higher rate of return for Pepsi vs. Coca-Cola to compensate for Pepsi's higher leverage and likely greater future demands for additional borrowing.

In the trailing 12 months, Pepsi has issued $16.5 billion in new debt. Compare that to Coca-Cola, with $5.6 billion in new debt issued. This indicates that Pepsi’s higher leverage is more a byproduct of operational needs than deliberate capital strategy. Combined with higher reinvestment needs, this leverage makes sustaining or growing the dividend more challenging relative to Coca-Cola.

Capital structure winner: Coca-Cola

CapEx Intensity and Dividend Sustainability

A consequence of the differences in business models and capital structures between the two companies is that Pepsi has greater capital expenditure requirements for continuing operations. Pepsi has to divert more of its revenue, operating income, and cash flows toward maintaining its assets than Coca-Cola has to, as shown in the table.

Metric PEP KO
CapEx/Revenue 5.4% 4.2%
CapEx/Operating Income 35.9% 13.4%
CapEx/CFO 42.3% 26.7%
Dividends/Net Income 105% 65%

These additional capital requirements have also put more pressure on the sustainability of Pepsi's dividend. In the trailing 12 months, Pepsi's dividends paid have exceeded net income (payout ratio of 105%), although they're still less than cash flow from operations. Pepsi's business model, increasing debt load, and capital expenditure requirements, along with a dividend already being stretched, bring into serious question the ability to continue expanding dividends at historical rates. Coca-Cola, on the other hand, has lower CapEx as a percent of revenues, operating income, and cash flow from operations, and has a very manageable dividend payout ratio of 65%.

CapEx intensity winner: Coca-Cola

Dividend sustainability winner: Coca-Cola

Historical Performance Comparison

The two stocks tend to perform similarly, with nearly identical betas (0.42 for PEP and 0.39 for KO based on Yahoo Finance data). Pepsi seems to have been more sensitive to increasing interest rates than Coca-Cola, which makes sense bearing in mind the discussion in the previous section. Pepsi has been borrowing more to fund capital expenditures. Additionally, both companies will have to continue refinancing low-interest debt issued during the low-rate environment in the early 2020s, but this will likely hurt Pepsi more than Coke, since PEP has a higher debt-to-equity ratio (25.2% vs. 15.6%).

These two stocks probably won't match an S&P index fund in raw returns, but that's not the value they bring to a portfolio. Most investors are risk-averse, which means that they have a preference for predictability and low volatility. In theory, they are willing to trade return for less risk. That is precisely what stocks like KO and PEP offer. Even if their raw returns fall short of an S&P index fund like SPY, they can still generate positive alpha if their returns exceed what would be expected based on their level of risk.

Valuation

Since both KO and PEP are dividend kings (each consistently growing their dividends for the last 50 years or more), this comparison presents a perfect opportunity to use dividend discount valuation models. The current forward dividend for KO is $2.04 per share (2.89% yield), while for PEP it's $5.69 per share (3.97% yield). However, with a higher return on equity and a higher retention ratio, KO has far more leeway to grow dividends more aggressively than PEP has.

Not all great businesses are great stocks, and vice versa. Superior business models and capital structures only matter if they translate into higher risk-adjusted returns. They inform the inputs and assumptions of the following valuation analysis, but alone they don't determine which stock is a better buy. So although KO is, in my view, a better business than PEP is, that alone does not necessarily mean that it is the more attractively valued of the two stocks.

PEP Stock Valuation

For Pepsi, I use an H-model to value the stock. Just to explain how an H model works, it assumes an initial rate of dividend growth that then slowly decays to a terminal rate over time. In my valuation model for PEP, I used an initial dividend growth rate of 5.5% decaying to a terminal growth rate of 3% over 10 years. Why these values? I believe in the near term Pepsi's dividend growth rate will decelerate slightly relative to historical growth rates. PEP's dividend has increased from $4.0225 in 2020 to $5.6225 in 2025, a 6.9% compound annual growth rate. Since 2000, Pepsi has actually increased its dividend at a very impressive 9.7% compound annual growth rate (from $0.55 per share in 2000 to $5.6225 in 2025). That growth has come as a result of globalization tailwinds that are unlikely to be repeated over the next 25 years, in my view. Furthermore, Pepsi's high payout ratio and capital requirements and growing debt burden are likely to be headwinds for dividend growth.

Generally in discounted cash flow or discounted dividend models, a terminal growth rate in line with nominal GDP growth in the markets in which the company operates is used to determine a terminal value for the stock. This is because in the long run, cash flows cannot grow faster than the underlying economy that funds them. For PEP, I used a 3% value as the terminal growth rate since the company operates in both developed and emerging markets (developed markets have nominal growth rates around 2.5-3%, with emerging market growth rates a bit higher). But PEP also has to invest more to fund potential growth, which places a lower ceiling on its long-run growth potential in my view.

To discount PEP's projected future dividends, we have to determine values for beta, equity risk premium, and risk-free rate. Starting with beta: we want a value that reflects future risk rather than recent historical risk. PEP's beta is unusually low at the moment (3Y daily beta only 0.287 as shown in the chart below). I conservatively use a value of 0.6 to reflect the historical norm from 2017-2023, rather than leaning too heavily on recent data.

I also conservatively use an equity risk premium of 6%, higher than the current implied equity risk premium, and a risk-free rate of 4.2% (the current 10-year treasury yield rounded up to the nearest tenth of a percent). These assumptions give us a discount rate of 7.8% using the CAPM model (6%*0.6 + 4.2%). The sanity check with the required rate of return is whether a rational investor would be satisfied with this return given the risk of buying the stock. A 7.8% required rate of return for PEP checks out in my view - it is a satisfactory rate given how PEP's stock behaves historically.

An H model using these assumptions produces a fair value per share of $137 compared to a current price per share of ~ $146, suggesting that shares are currently about 6.6% overvalued.

KO Stock Valuation

To value KO shares, I used a slightly more complex model called a 3-stage dividend discount model. I believe KO has more leeway to expand dividends in the short term and likely has a higher potential growth in earnings in the near term than PEP does.

KO has grown its dividend from $1.64 in 2020 to $2.04 in 2025, a 4.5% compound annual growth rate. Since 2002, the dividend has grown from $0.40 per share to $2.04 per share, a 7.5% CAGR.

In stage 1, I assume 3 years of dividend growth at 6.0% per year. Recently, earnings per share for KO have increased significantly while dividend yield has slightly decreased, as shown in the chart below. That suggests a short-term capacity to continue expanding dividend payments at a higher rate than in recent years.

In stage 2, I assume a decay from 6.0% growth in dividends to 3.5% growth over 8 years. Why? Again, I believe that KO has a longer runway and more robust ability to continue expanding dividends than PEP has.

In stage 3, I assume 3.5% dividend growth in perpetuity. I believe a slightly higher perpetual growth rate is warranted for KO than for PEP, since KO can achieve growth without as much capital investment as PEP requires. So I believe that KO can capture increasing purchasing power in emerging markets relatively easily, without needing huge investment to expand capacity.

Similar to PEP, KO stock is at a historically low beta at the moment. We want to account for future risk, not recent past risk. Therefore, I use a beta value of 0.5, which is fairly average for the stock over the last 10 years:

To get the discount rate, I calculated the cost of equity using CAPM. Beta of 0.5, with the same equity risk premium (6%) and risk-free rate (4.2%) that we used for PEP yields a required rate of return of 7.2%. Again, the sanity check: would a rational investor buying KO stock be satisfied with a rate of return of 7.2% given the risk associated with the stock? This discount rate passes the sanity check for me - for the risk inherent in KO's stock, 7.2% is a satisfactory rate of return.

Under these assumptions, the three-stage dividend discount model gives us a fair value for KO shares of $78, a 9.2% premium over the current share price of $70.51.

Relative Valuation Comparison

Metric PEP KO
Forward P/E 16.34 21.7
PEG Ratio 2.82 2.20
Price/Sales 2.08 6.38
Price/Book 9.87 9.71
EV/EBITDA 16.48

18.02

On relative valuation, the comparison is a bit of a wash. PEP is cheaper based on forward P/E, price-to-sales, and EV-to-EBITDA, while KO is cheaper by PEG ratio and price-to-book value. KO's higher P/E and P/S are explained by higher short-term growth expectations and a capital-light business structure, while PEP's higher PEG ratio is also explained by lower short-term growth expectations. Overall, the relative valuation metrics don't tell the story as clearly as the absolute valuation models based on discounted dividends, in my view.

Q3 Earnings (And What to Expect in Q4 Earnings)

PEP's Q3 Earnings

There are a few themes to highlight from Pepsi's Q3 earnings.

The importance of product innovation

CEO Ramon Laguarta heavily emphasized product innovation, especially with regard to health-conscious consumers. The company is focusing on snacks with higher protein and fiber content and on creating snacks using healthier oils like avocado oil. These innovations are expected to be margin-accretive - although they cost more to make, they also sell at higher price points. Management refers to this as their "permissible" subcategory (these are snacks that health-conscious consumers can still enjoy).

Margin Expansion

While tariffs hit margins in Q3, Laguarta says that Pepsi is seeing an expansion of margin again (in Q4) to complete a positive margin expansion for the year. In the next earnings call, investors should look for confirmation of Q4 2025 margin expansion and guidance for continued margin expansion into 2026. Price increases in emerging markets are also expected to be a driver of margin expansion going forward. Margin expansion will determine if Pepsi can continue increasing dividends at near the rate of the prior 5 years.

Overall, I would keep an eye on Pepsi's dividend payout ratio and whether it begins declining to a more sustainable level. Look for signs of very short-term deceleration in dividend growth, as that would imply that the short-term dividend growth rates I used in Pepsi's valuation may be on the high side.

KO Q3 Earnings

Below are some key themes from KO's Q3 earnings call.

Refranchising strategy nearly complete

Coca-Cola's refranchising strategy is in its final stages, according to Quincey, with India and Africa remaining as the major final steps to completion of the strategy. Refranchising is the selling of the capital-intensive bottling operations to allow Coke to focus on the concentrate business. This has allowed the operating margin to expand from 26.5% in 2017 toward the mid-30s currently. In Q4 earnings, investors should look for continued margin expansion as a result of continued efficiency gains from refranchising.

Coca-Cola Zero Sugar and Diet Coke reaccelerating

The non-sugar Coca-Cola drinks have experienced reaccelerating demand according to management. This is an important development as it counteracts the secular decline in demand for sugary drinks. Investors should look for continued volume growth in Coke Zero and Diet Coke in Q4, as it would reinforce durable demand and limit downside risk from decreasing demand for original Coca-Cola.

International Expansion

International markets remain one of Coca-Cola’s most important long-term growth drivers, with management continuing to emphasize volume growth in emerging markets where consumption is still well below levels in developed markets. Because KO’s concentrate-based model scales without heavy capital investment, international growth can be highly margin-accretive. In Q4 earnings, investors should watch organic volume growth outside North America and management’s commentary on India and Africa as refranchising nears completion.

Overall, the key drivers to look out for in KO's Q4 earnings are margin expansion due to refranchising, continued acceleration in non-sugar beverages, and an increase in international sales. These are at the core of Coca-Cola's current growth strategy.

Conclusion - Which Stock to Buy?
